title: A Covenant, Not a Contract
subtitle: What our culture's prenups reveal about marriage — and the better story Christ tells
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: A celebrity engagement put prenuptial agreements back in the headlines, and most of our culture read it as simple wisdom. But underneath the news were two completely different stories about what marriage is — and most people cannot tell you which one they are living in until a crisis forces the question.
body: A celebrity engagement made the news not long ago, and the detail that drew the most attention was not the ring or the wedding plans. It was the prenuptial agreement, and the announcement that the groom-to-be had "agreed to the terms." Much of the public read this as a kind of romantic gesture — proof of his devotion. To most of our culture, a carefully negotiated prenup is simply what wise, modern people do before they marry.

I want to be careful here, because I do not know this couple and I do not know the terms of their agreement. But the conversation around it surfaced something worth examining — not about them, but about us. Underneath the headlines were two completely different stories about what marriage is. And most people cannot tell you which story they are living in until a crisis forces the question.
## Two Stories About Marriage

The first story is that marriage is a contract. A contract is an arrangement between two parties, each protecting his or her own interests, with terms defining what each owes the other and an exit clause for when those terms are not met. A contract is, by design, a way of limiting your exposure. It assumes the relationship may end and that protecting yourself is only prudent.

The second story is that marriage is a covenant. A covenant is not a way of limiting your exposure; it is the opposite. It is the binding of two lives into one, with no exit clause, because the thing that holds it together is not performance but a promise. This is the story Scripture tells from its first pages.

> Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife, and they shall become one flesh.
— Genesis 2:24

"One flesh" is not the language of a partnership agreement. You cannot draw up terms for becoming one flesh. You cannot negotiate the fair division of one body. The image is total self-giving — two people so joined that what belongs to one belongs to the other, with nothing held back and nothing kept in reserve.

A word of honesty is needed here. There is no scriptural prohibition against a prenuptial agreement. The Bible nowhere forbids one, and we should not bind a conscience the Word leaves free. There are situations — children from a previous marriage, a family business, responsibilities a person did not choose — where godly people weigh these things and land in different places. So the question Scripture presses is not really about the document. It is about the story underneath it. A signed agreement can sit on top of a covenant heart, and a beautiful ceremony can hide a contract heart. What God searches is which of the two stories you are actually living in.
## Where the Covenant Comes From

Why would anyone live that way? Why surrender every protection, hold nothing back, and bind yourself to another person for life with no way out? The answer is that the covenant of marriage was never meant to stand on its own. It was always pointing to something beyond itself.

> "Therefore a man shall leave his father and mother and hold fast to his wife, and the two shall become one flesh." This mystery is profound, and I am saying that it refers to Christ and the church.
— Ephesians 5:31–32

Paul takes the oldest description of marriage and tells us what it was always about. Marriage is a living picture of the way Christ loves His church. And that love is the furthest thing from a contract. Christ did not enter a carefully negotiated arrangement with His people, hedged against the risk of our failure. He bound Himself to a people who brought nothing to the table, who could not perform, who had no assets worth protecting, and who betrayed Him again and again — and He held to the covenant anyway, at infinite cost to Himself.

Richard Sibbes put it simply: "Christ betroths the church to himself in mercy." Not in exchange for performance. In mercy. That is the model marriage was made to reflect: a faithfulness that does not depend on the other person earning it. It is no accident that God so often describes His own relationship to His people as a marriage, or that their wandering grieves Him the way betrayal grieves a faithful spouse. The whole Bible is, in one sense, the story of a God who refuses to protect Himself from the cost of loving us.
## The Story Your Counselee Is Already Living

Many counselees have absorbed the contract story without ever choosing it. They learned it from a parent's divorce, from a betrayal they never recovered from, from a culture that calls self-protection wisdom. They are not cynics. They are afraid. And the prenup, whether it is written on paper or only carried in the heart, is fear wearing the costume of prudence.

You do not help such a person by scolding them for their fear or arguing with them about celebrities. You help them by surfacing the fear gently, and then telling them a better story — the only story strong enough to make covenant love possible. A person will risk being fully known, with no exit plan, only after they have first been loved that way. And that love has a name.
## Questions Worth Considering

Whether you are a counselor sitting with someone who is wrestling with marriage, or simply willing to examine your own heart, these are the kinds of questions that bring the real story to the surface. They are not meant to be fired off in sequence like an interrogation. They are questions to hold in your mind and ask gently, as doors open.

*When you imagine a healthy marriage, what does it look like? What would have to happen for you to feel you needed to leave?* Questions like these surface what a person actually believes marriage is, long before a single verse is opened.

*What are you most afraid would happen if you held nothing back — no exit plan, no safety net? Where does that fear come from?* Get underneath the logic of self-protection and you will usually find an old wound: a trust that was broken, a marriage that failed, a quiet conviction that no one is finally safe to be fully known by.

*If marriage cost you everything and gave you nothing back, would it still be worth it?* The answer tells you what a person is really trusting in — financial security, independence, control, reputation. The idol will name itself if you ask the right question.

*Have you ever felt that God was holding something back from you, protecting Himself from the cost of loving you?* How a person experiences God's faithfulness and how they approach human intimacy are almost never unrelated. Someone who does not trust God's covenant love will struggle to live it out toward a spouse.

*If God had entered a prenup with us — protecting Himself against the risk of our failure — where would that leave you and me?* This is the question that turns the whole conversation toward the gospel.
## The Love That Makes Self-Protection Unnecessary

In the end, no one is argued into covenant marriage. People are not persuaded to stop protecting themselves; they are freed from the need to. And that freedom comes from one place only: an encounter with the love of a God who entered covenant with us at infinite personal cost and has never once moved to protect Himself from us.

He knew exactly what loving us would cost. He saw the betrayal coming. And He held fast anyway. That is the marriage every human marriage was made to picture, and it is the only love strong enough to make us willing to hold nothing back.
"Christ betroths the church to himself in mercy." Help a person feel the weight of that love, and the exit clauses quietly lose their appeal.

title: Jesus, Breaker of Boundaries
subtitle: Better than boundaries — limitations, love, and wisdom
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Counselees often ask for help “setting healthy boundaries.” But Scripture offers something deeper and more demanding than a self-drawn circle — limitations, love, and wisdom, and a Lord who broke down every dividing wall.
body: A frequent request from counselees struggling with difficult relationships or the strain of managing life is for help "setting healthy boundaries." The language of "boundaries" has become a fixture of the Christian vocabulary, and drawing them is widely regarded as a mark of wisdom and maturity. Boundaries are often described as defining what is yours and what is not, so that you can "own your life and choices"—a self-drawn circle enclosing you and whatever you value, with others invited to draw their own. It is an appealing and remarkably flexible idea.

Consider the situations in which people reach for boundaries: physical exhaustion or emotional overwhelm; chronic anger or resentment; an inability to say "no"; poor habits of body and soul; an inability to speak the truth in love; relationships without mutual care and service; feeling controlled by the opinions and desires of others; being driven mainly by guilt or fear. These are real burdens, and the people who carry them are not wrong to long for relief. The question is whether "boundaries" is the answer Scripture actually gives.
## Is "Boundaries" a Biblical Idea?

Some episodes in our Lord's life seem to commend the wisdom of limits. He tended His body's needs, sleeping peacefully through a storm (Mark 4:37–39). He guarded His communion with the Father, withdrawing to pray though the crowds pressed in (Luke 5:15–16). He refused the agenda others pressed on Him, declining to be made an earthly king (John 6:15). And though He came to lay down His life, He did so according to the Father's appointed plan, not the will of men, at times withdrawing from those who sought His harm (Luke 9:10).

And yet the deeper truth is that Jesus, from first to last, broke down every dividing wall (Eph. 2:14). He crossed the boundary of heaven itself to become man and dwell among us. The only person who ever had the right to wall Himself off from sinners instead ate with them, touched and was touched by lepers and prostitutes and the unclean, spoke with a Samaritan woman of loose reputation, overturned the temple tables, forgave sins, healed on the Sabbath, and declared that He and the Father are one. He commended a Samaritan who entangled himself in a stranger's trouble rather than minding his own affairs, and He charged His followers to love their enemies rather than fortify themselves against them. This is not the portrait of a man managing a tidy, self-protective circle.

So we should be cautious about baptizing "boundaries" as a biblical command. It is, at best, a modern category we must hold up to the light of God's Word—and at worst, a Christianized version of the autonomous self.
## You Are Not Your Own

Here is where the boundaries model most needs examining. Its foundational instinct is ownership: define what is yours, take back control, "own your life." But Scripture confronts the believer with a different and better word: "You are not your own, for you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body" (1 Cor. 6:19–20). "None of us lives to himself… we are the Lord's" (Rom. 14:7–8). As the old catechism confesses, our only comfort in life and in death is that "I am not my own, but belong—body and soul, in life and in death—to my faithful Savior, Jesus Christ."

This changes everything. The Christian's life is not a circle to be defended but a life that has been purchased, claimed, and now belongs to Another. Our chief end is not to secure and curate what is ours, but to glorify God and to enjoy Him forever. A counselee who learns only to draw firmer lines around the self may simply trade one bondage for another—exchanging the tyranny of others' demands for the tyranny of self-rule.

There is a better way, and it is more demanding, more nuanced, and more faithful than a single word can hold. Scripture braids together three strands—**limitations, love, and wisdom**—and it is in their interplay that holiness and health grow. (This three-fold framework follows Brenda Pauken, ["Better Than Boundaries"](https://www.biblicalcounselingcoalition.org/2021/02/05/better-than-boundaries/), Biblical Counseling Coalition, 2021).
## Limitations

We are creatures, not the Creator. God has appointed limits to our time, strength, knowledge, and ability; our bodies are subject to weakness, decay, and death. To rage against the realities of our finitude—to refuse sleep, rest, nourishment, and ordinary care—is not devotion but a quiet contempt for the way God made us. Stewarding the gift of life means honoring the limits the Lord has built into it.

To honor our limits is also to confess that we are not sovereign—God is. There are no outcomes we can guarantee. When a hard relationship does not change, it is easy to conclude we must have failed; but we are not called to produce the change, only to labor faithfully and leave the increase to God (1 Cor. 3:6–7). His sovereignty frees us from the crushing weight of results that were never ours to carry.

Honoring limits will also require saying "no" to some genuinely good things that are simply not ours to do in this season. If that proves nearly impossible, the cause may not be love at all but people-pleasing, a savior complex, an identity built on usefulness, or a quiet failure to trust the God who actually governs the world. Our motives need rigorous, prayerful examination, for the heart is deceitful above all things (Jer. 17:9).
## Love

A focus on limits, left to itself, can curdle into the very self-love, self-focus, and self-protection that our sin already inclines us toward. So limitation must be governed by love—love that is the opposite of a defended boundary, a giving of oneself, by grace, beyond what we thought we could bear. Such love does not originate in us; it flows from the wildly unbalanced love we have received from God in Christ: "We love because he first loved us" (1 John 4:19).

But we must let Scripture, not the therapeutic culture, define love—because it is easy to baptize sentiment or self-interest with that holy word. And here our Lord is startlingly concrete: **"If you love me, you will keep my commandments"** (John 14:15; cf. 14:21; 15:10; 1 John 5:3). Jesus does not leave love to be defined by feeling, by self-expression, or by what protects us. He weds love to obedience—to the commandments of God. And the whole law, in turn, is summed up in love to God and love to neighbor (Matt. 22:37–40), so that the commandments are not the enemy of love but its very shape and content. To ask "How do I love this difficult person?" is therefore to ask "What does God's revealed will require of me toward them?"—not "Where do I draw the line?"

This reframes everything the boundaries model tries to address. Scripture never moves from the command to love to a psychological category like "boundaries"; it presses us instead to ask what it looks like to love a hard person according to God's commandments.

And godly love, defined by the law, is robust. It confronts evil rather than ignoring it (Lev. 19:17; Matt. 18:15). It perseveres and forgives (1 Cor. 13:7; Col. 3:13). It refuses to repay evil for evil, blessing even those who persecute (1 Pet. 3:9; Rom. 12:17–21). It does not interfere with God's just pattern of sowing and reaping (Gal. 6:7). It is willing to risk another's displeasure rather than please, placate, and pretend. It will not do for others what they can and should do for themselves, when "helping" only feeds laziness, entitlement, and self-absorption (2 Thess. 3:10). And it rescues people from their folly, as Abigail's intervention rescued both David and Nabal (1 Sam. 25:3–35).

Because we are secure in God's love for us in Christ, we are freed to seek another's true good ahead of our own comfort—just as Christ "made himself nothing," took the limits of a servant, and laid down His rights to save us (Phil. 2:5–8). And precisely because love is defined by God's commandments and aims at another's good, the most loving act toward an unrepentant or abusive person may be to deny him the opportunity to go on sinning against us. Limiting contact, in such cases, is not the self-protective circle of the boundaries model; it is obedience—a refusal to enable sin, a guarding against further harm, and an act of hope for the relationship's eventual restoration through repentance.
## Wisdom

Honoring our limits and loving as the law commands is never formulaic; it is the work of wisdom, which holds the two together under the fear of the Lord. Wisdom weighs our call to love as God loves, our creaturely limits in this season, the particular temptations to sin and self that each of us carries, and the actual person and need set before us.

Wisdom is the labor of a lifetime, but the means of grace are appointed for it. First, sit under the whole counsel of God's Word, not a favored slice of it, so that you are not left with lopsided answers. Look for the tensions Scripture holds together—justice and mercy, God's sovereignty and our responsibility, our limits and our calling to love—and let them keep you from simplistic solutions that flatter the self.

Second, do not go it alone. God has given the church. Mature brothers and sisters can expose our blind spots, lend perspective our own hearts are too biased to see, and bear our burdens with us (Gal. 6:2).

Finally, commune with God Himself. Wisdom is not finally a set of propositions but a knowing of the Lord (Prov. 9:10). What the struggling counselee most needs is not a static formula extracted from God but a living walk with Him. Our relationships are not problems to be solved and sealed off behind well-drawn lines; they are the very ground on which, by the Spirit, we are conformed to Christ—reflecting His patience, His long-suffering, and His relentless, pursuing love for sinners like us.

title: Grace in the Furnace
subtitle: How suffering shapes us into vessels of Christ
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: The potter does not despise the cracked clay. He sees in every fracture an opportunity — a place where his hands can press deeper, where the fire can do its refining work.
body: The potter does not despise the cracked clay. He sees in every fracture an opportunity — a place where his hands can press deeper, where the fire can do its refining work. Biblical counseling begins here: not with solutions, but with a theology of the kiln.

Romans 8:28 is often quoted as a comfort verse, deployed quickly in the presence of grief as though the pain were an embarrassment to be expedited. But Paul's argument is richer and more demanding. He does not promise that all things are good; he promises that God works all things together for good to those who are called according to his purpose.

That purpose (prothesis) is not vague. Paul defines it immediately: to be conformed to the image of his Son. Conformity to Christ is the furnace's aim. The counselor's task, then, is not to remove the heat but to help the sufferer understand what the heat is for.

This does not minimize pain. It dignifies it. When we tell a grieving mother that her loss is purposeless randomness, we offer her nothing but a cold universe. When we tell her it is being shaped by a Father who gave his own Son, we give her something to stand on even in the ash.

title: Social Media and the Distracted Soul
subtitle: Reclaiming attention as an act of worship
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Attention is not a neutral faculty. Scripture treats it as a worshipful act. What we behold, we become — and the architecture of social media is designed to fragment beholding into an infinite scroll of competing claimants for the soul.
body: Attention is not a neutral faculty. Scripture treats it as a worshipful act. What we behold, we become — and the architecture of social media is designed to fragment beholding into an infinite scroll of competing claimants for the soul.

Psalm 115 draws the oldest connection between idolatry and transformation: those who make idols become like them. The idol of the social feed is not stone but pixel — yet the dynamic is identical. It promises significance (likes), belonging (followers), and identity (personal brand). These are not trivial desires. They are the desires that only Christ can fill.

Biblical counseling in this space must resist two failures: the moralistic (just stop using it) and the therapeutic (set healthy screen limits). Both treat the behavior without the heart. The deeper question is always: what does this person believe social media can give them that they cannot find in Christ?

The goal is not Luddism. It is liturgical reordering — helping the counselee rebuild a life of attention whose center of gravity is Word, prayer, and embodied community, rather than the dopamine pulse of notification.

title: Come to Me, All Who Labor
subtitle: The yoke of Christ as rest, not burden
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: The invitation of Matthew 11:28 is the strangest paradox in the Gospels. Jesus calls the weary to take on a yoke. Rest is found not in the removal of burden but in the exchange of burdens.
body: The invitation of Matthew 11:28 is the strangest paradox in the Gospels. Jesus calls the weary to take on a yoke. Rest is found not in the removal of burden but in the exchange of burdens — from the crushing weight of self-justification to the gentle load of grace.

First-century listeners heard yoke as a rabbinic metaphor for Torah observance and discipleship. Every teacher had a yoke — his interpretive school, his demands, his system. Jesus is not abolishing yokes; he is contrasting his yoke with every other. I am gentle and lowly in heart, he says. No rabbi before him ever described himself that way.

The weariness Jesus addresses is not primarily physical. It is the exhaustion of performing for an audience that is never satisfied. The yoke of Christ is a transfer of jurisdiction: from the court of human opinion to the verdict already spoken at the cross.

In counseling, we meet this weariness constantly. The anxious man who cannot rest because he cannot prove his worth. The perfectionistic woman who serves God as though grace had a co-pay. The gospel addresses not their behavior but their court. Christ has already spoken the verdict: Beloved.

title: The Counselor as Barnabas
subtitle: Encouragement as a theological act
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Barnabas means son of encouragement — yet that title was given, not born. His name was Joseph. The church renamed him for what he did, and what he did was see potential in people others had written off. This is the counselor's vocation.
body: Barnabas means son of encouragement — yet that title was given, not born. His name was Joseph. The church renamed him for what he did, and what he did was see potential in people others had written off. This is the counselor's vocation.

The Greek word paraklesis — encouragement, exhortation, comfort — shares its root with parakletos, the word Jesus uses for the Holy Spirit. This is not accidental. Encouragement, at its depth, is the Spirit's work expressed through human presence. The counselor does not manufacture hope; he mediates it.

Barnabas appears at two critical junctures: he vouches for the feared Saul to a suspicious Jerusalem church, and he later refuses to abandon John Mark when Paul would. In both cases, he extends trust to the person others found disqualifying. This is not naivety. It is a theology of second chances rooted in the God who restored Peter.

Good biblical counseling looks for what God is still doing in a person, not merely cataloging what has broken. It asks: what does redemption look like here? What does resurrection look like, in this particular life, at this particular moment?

title: Anxiety and the Sovereignty of God
subtitle: Why Philippians 4:6 is not a command to simply calm down
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Philippians 4:6 is one of the most quoted and most misapplied verses in pastoral ministry. Do not be anxious about anything is read as a command to stop feeling anxious — as though anxiety were simply a moral failure correctable by willpower.
body: Philippians 4:6 is one of the most quoted and most misapplied verses in pastoral ministry. Do not be anxious about anything is read as a command to stop feeling anxious — as though anxiety were simply a moral failure correctable by willpower. Paul's meaning is richer and more merciful than that.

The word translated be anxious (merimnao) carries the sense of a divided mind — a heart pulled in multiple directions by competing concerns. Paul is not condemning the anxious person; he is diagnosing a condition and prescribing a reorientation: In everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God.

Notice the structure: the antidote to divided attention is not focused attention on one's own feelings but directed speech toward God. The anxious person is not told to think less about their problem; they are told to bring the problem to the One who holds all things. Prayer is the transfer of burden.

This is why the passage ends not with your anxiety will disappear but with the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds. The peace comes after the prayer — and it is a peace that outpaces comprehension, meaning the circumstances may not change, but the orientation of the heart does.

title: Help! I'm Spending Too Much Time on Social Media!
subtitle: Walking wisely in a culture engineered for distraction
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: You didn't plan to spend an hour on TikTok. You never do. But the algorithm is patient. Few passages speak more directly to this than Ephesians 5:8–17.
body: You did not plan to spend an hour on TikTok. You never do. But the algorithm is patient, and it seems that it knows you better than you know yourself. If you are a Christian with a smartphone, this article is for you.

Few passages speak more directly to it than Ephesians 5:8–17. Written to believers living in the spiritually dark and sexually permissive city of Ephesus, Paul's words cut to the heart of what it means to walk faithfully in a culture bent on distraction and indulgence.
## Three Key Exhortations
Three exhortations emerge from this passage that speak directly to the challenge of social media:
Do not partner with sin (vv. 7–8) — instead, walk in the light (v. 9). Discern what is pleasing to the Lord (v. 10). Walk wisely, redeeming your time, "because the days are evil" (v. 16).
## Do Not Partner with Sin

Algorithms are engineered to entice — to surface content that is sexually charged, that stokes envy, or that keeps you scrolling for one more hit of outrage or entertainment. When you indulge a creator's sinful content, you are not a passive observer. You are contributing to their platform and livelihood. In a very real sense, you are partnering with them in their sin.

> "The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately sick; who can understand it?"
— Jeremiah 17:9
## Be Discerning

Social media has a remarkable ability to dull our spiritual senses over time. Even seemingly harmless content can quietly crowd out the things of God when consumed without discretion. An unchecked diet of partisan news can shift our gaze from the eternal to the anxious, training us to think of ourselves as citizens of a political tribe rather than citizens of the Kingdom.
## Redeem the Time
Before you open an app, ask yourself honestly: How will this end? An hour from now, will I be more joyful? More prayerful? More ready to serve my neighbor and love my Savior?

> "Look carefully then how you walk, not as unwise but as wise, making the best use of the time, because the days are evil."
— Ephesians 5:15–16

God has purposes for your one life. You are called to carry the good news of the gospel to the people around you, to love your family, to build up the church. Every hour surrendered to an algorithm is an hour not given to those things. The days are indeed evil — but they are also short. Use them well.

title: Conquering Anger by GRACE
subtitle: Remembering what God has already done in Christ
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Sinful anger is an emotion rooted in pride, selfishness, or a desire for revenge. It manifests as outbursts, bitterness, and malice — what Scripture calls "wrath" or "rage."
body: ## What Is Sinful Anger?
Sinful anger is an emotion rooted in pride, selfishness, or a desire for revenge. It manifests as outbursts, bitterness, and malice — what Scripture often calls "wrath" or "rage."

> "What causes quarrels and what causes fights among you? Is it not this, that your passions are at war within you? You desire and do not have, so you murder. You covet and cannot obtain, so you fight and quarrel."
— James 4:1–2
## How Is This Different from God's Anger?
God's anger is perfectly justified and perfectly controlled. Human anger is frequently sinful, prone to foolishness, and often leads to evil action.
## Guidelines for Overcoming Sinful Anger
### 1. Remember That God Is in Control

> "Our God is in the heavens; he does all that he pleases."
— Psalm 115:3

> "No temptation has overtaken you that is not common to man. God is faithful, and he will not let you be tempted beyond your ability, but with the temptation he will also provide the way of escape."
— 1 Corinthians 10:13
### 2. Remember That You Are Not God

> "Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is written, Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord."
— Romans 12:19
### 3. Remember Who You Are in Christ

> "Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come."
— 2 Corinthians 5:17
### 4. Remember God's Graciousness to You — and Pass It On

> "Let all bitterness and wrath and anger and clamor and slander be put away from you, along with all malice. Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you."
— Ephesians 4:31–32

The Parable of the Unforgiving Servant powerfully illustrates this point. A servant forgiven an incalculable debt turns and refuses to forgive a fellow servant a trivial one. The grace we have received is the measure by which we are called to extend grace to others.

title: Emotions in Biblical Counseling
subtitle: Letting Scripture inform our feelings, not the other way around
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: Emotions are a window into a person's heart, enabling a counselor to gauge spiritual maturity, integrity, and the fruit of the Spirit.
body: Emotions are a window into a person's heart, enabling a counselor to gauge spiritual maturity, integrity, and the fruit of the Spirit. Counselors should allow emotions to surface freely, while taking care not to encourage or validate those that are sinful. The fruits of the Spirit listed in Galatians 5:22–23 serve as the primary gauge of righteous emotion.

> "The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately sick; who can understand it?"
— Jeremiah 17:9

Robert Robinson recognized this same reality when he wrote in 1758, "Prone to wander, Lord, I feel it, prone to leave the God I love." What Robinson confessed, genuine believers across the centuries have echoed — our hearts, as the seat of our emotions, require constant renewal from the Lord.
## The Spirit's Work in the Heart

Counselees are ultimately helpless apart from the Spirit's work in their lives. When a counselee cannot seem to gain control over lust, anger, or similar besetting emotions, this may indicate they are not yet united to Christ.

> "And such were some of you. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God."
— 1 Corinthians 6:11
## Scripture Over Feelings

We live in a therapeutic culture with deep roots in treating experiential sense perception as the primary guide in life. Believers struggling with sinful emotions must be taught that the objective Word of God holds that place instead. Scripture ought to inform our feelings — not the other way around.

> "Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth."
— Colossians 3:2
## Scripture as the Instrument of Change

Scripture is ultimately the key instrument of heart change. I once counseled a man struggling with anger and a retaliatory spirit in a genuinely difficult work environment. We read Ephesians 4:30–32 together, and the change evident in his life over the following month was extraordinary — a clear testimony to the Spirit's work.
This is why, moment by moment, we return to Christ in faith — empty-handed — trusting that He alone can fill all in all, and that His Spirit is the one building us into the image of His beloved Son.

title: There Is No Seam
subtitle: The Trinitarian Beauty of Ephesians 1
author: Jeff Johnson
excerpt: In Greek, it is a single sentence. Twelve verses. Paul builds it like a vault of arches climbing toward heaven — teaching that salvation is Trinitarian from beginning to end, and that there is no seam through which a believer can fall.
body: Some passages of Scripture are quiet — small, intimate, almost whispered. Others are cathedrals.
Ephesians 1:3–14 is a cathedral.

In Greek, it is a single sentence. Twelve verses. Two hundred and two words. Paul builds it like a vault of arches climbing toward heaven, and the architecture is not accidental. He is not constructing an abstract theological treatise. He is teaching the church that salvation is Trinitarian from beginning to end. Every stage. Every moment. Every thread.
And the practical implications for the trembling believer are staggering.
## The Architecture
The passage divides with unmistakable precision. Paul moves through three movements, each devoted to one Person of the Triune God:
**The Father** (vv. 3–6) — who chose us before the foundation of the world, predestined us for adoption, and made us accepted in the Beloved.
**The Son** (vv. 7–12) — in whom we have redemption through His blood, the forgiveness of trespasses, and the inheritance promised to those who trust Him.
**The Spirit** (vv. 13–14) — who seals us upon belief and serves as the down payment of our coming inheritance.
Father chooses. Son redeems. Spirit seals.

This is not three Gods doing three things. This is one God — Father, Son, and Spirit — whose every Person has personally invested Himself in the rescue of your soul. There is no moment in the salvation process that is impersonal. There is no stage that is mechanical. There is no link in the chain that is staffed by some lesser deputy. The entire Godhead has staked itself on the rescue of your soul, *"to the praise of His glorious grace"* (v. 6).
## No Seam Through Which You Can Fall
This is where the doctrine becomes pastoral.
If you are a believer in Jesus Christ, the joint and inseparable work of Father, Son, and Spirit means there is no seam through which you can fall.
Read that again.

The Father chose you. The Son bought you. The Spirit sealed you. These are not three separate transactions in some divine bureaucracy where one Person might lose track of you while another isn't paying attention. This is the unbreakable agreement of a Triune God who has set His love on you and will not let it go.
Paul says it plainly elsewhere:

> "Neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers, nor height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord."
— Romans 8:38–39

If you ever feel weak and wonder whether God truly loves you, Ephesians 1 invites you to see something almost too wonderful to believe: *the Father loves you with the same love with which He loves the Son.* Jesus Himself prays this in John 17:23 — *"that the world may know that you sent me and loved them even as you loved me."* Because you are united to the Son by faith, you are inseparable from the love of the Father.
And the Spirit — given to you the moment you believed — is the seal, the down payment, the *guarantee* of all that is yet to come.
## The Triune God Has Entered Your Condition
There is one more layer of beauty here, and it is the layer that lifts the head of the depressed and breaks the back of pride.
The Trinitarian God of Ephesians 1 is not a distant theological abstraction. He is not a doctrine to be debated in a classroom. He is the God who has, through the Son, entered into your condition.

> "He has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows."
— Isaiah 53:4

> "We do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but One who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin."
— Hebrews 4:15

The full range of human temptation, sadness, and weakness — He has been there. Not as an observer. As a Man. The Son who redeems you in Ephesians 1:7 is the same Son who wept at Lazarus's tomb, who sweat blood in Gethsemane, who cried out in dereliction on the cross. This is not a salvation administered from a safe distance. This is salvation carried out by a God who came down into the wreckage to lift you out of it.
## What Would Change?
So here is the question worth sitting with:
*What would it mean for you today if your worth were based not on what you have done, but on what God did through Christ?*
What would that do to your anxiety — the running tally of your failures, the dread that you have not measured up?
What would it do to your depression — the slow, gray conviction that you are alone, unloved, forgotten?
What would it do to sexual temptation — the lie that this small, secret pleasure is what your soul actually needs?
What would it do to your pride — the brittle structure you have built to convince yourself you are worth loving?
The Trinitarian salvation of Ephesians 1 does not whisper at these things. It thunders against them.
The Father has chosen you. The Son has redeemed you. The Spirit has sealed you.
*There is no seam.*

And the same God who arranged this rescue with such breathtaking architecture does not now stand back to let you wander through life unattended. He is with you. He is for you. And He has given you Himself — every Person of Himself — as the inheritance for which you wait.
*To the praise of His glorious grace.*
